elements.sent-2411-001: For noncovalent interactions, it is generally assumed that CCSD(T) is nearly the exact solution within the 1-particle basis set.
elements.sent-2411-002: For the S66 noncovalent interactions benchmark, we present for the majority of species CCSDT and CCSDT(Q) corrections with a polarized double-zeta basis set.
elements.sent-2411-003: For hydrogen bonds, pure London complexes, and mixed-influence complexes, CCSD(T) benefits from error cancellation between (usually repulsive) higher-order triples, $T_3 - (T)$, and (almost universally attractive) connected quadruples, (Q).
elements.sent-2411-004: For $\pi$-stacking complexes, this cancellation starts breaking down and CCSD(T) overbinds; CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$ corrects the problem at the expense of London complexes.
elements.sent-2411-005: A fairly simple two-parameter model predicts CCSDT(Q)--CCSD(T) differences to 0.01 kcal/mol RMS, requiring no calculations that scale more steeply than $O(N^7)$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The importance of noncovalent interactions in supramolecular chemistry, molecular recognition, and the structure of condensed matter hardly needs to be reiterated.
elements.sent-2411-007: As individual interactions are difficult to access experimentally, accurate ab initio wavefunction theory (WFT) calculations have become the primary source of training and validation data for more approximate approaches.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Opus301fig2.pdf} \caption{Box-and-whiskers plot of the total atomization energy contributions of higher-order corrections in the W4-17 dataset.\cite{jmlm273} Outer fences encompass 95\% of the set, inner fences 80\%, boxes 50\%. Vertical lines span from population minimum to maximum. Reprinted from Figure 2 in Ref.\cite{jmlm301}, Copyright Springer Nature.} \label{fig:opus301}
elements.sent-2411-008: Very recently, however, al-Hamdani \etal\cite{Al-Hamdani2021 showed that for ever larger $\pi$-stacking complexes, an increasingly larger gap opens between localized natural orbital CCSD(T)\cite{Nagy2019,local_corr_review2024 and fixed node-diffusion Monte Carlo (FN-DMC)\cite{Dubecky2016 results, which purportedly is more accurate (devoid of systematic error) even as it suffers from lower precision (greater stochastic error).}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Gr\"uneis and coworkers\cite{Grueneis2024 attributed the discrepancy to CCSD(T) overshooting the effect of triple excitations in systems with large polarizabilities, which they call the `infrared catastrophe'.\cite{GrueneisPRL It does appear from Fig. 1 in Ref.\citenum{Grueneis2024 that their CCSD(cT) approach,\cite{GrueneisPRL which can be seen as a noniterative approximation to CCSDT-2,\cite{Noga1987 addresses the problem.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: But this leaves the reader with as many questions as answers.
elements.sent-2411-011: What about fully iterative CCSDT? And do connected quadruples again disrupt agreement with FN-DMC? And how representative is this? Owing to the very steep computational cost scaling of approaches such as CCSDT, $O(n_{\rm occ ^3N_{\rm virt ^5)$ (where $n_{\rm occ}$ and $N_{\rm virt}$ are the numbers of occupied and virtual orbitals, respectively), and CCSDT(Q), $O(n_{\rm occ ^4N_{\rm virt ^5)$, post-CCSD(T) studies of noncovalent complexes are fairly scant,\cite{A24,Boese2013,Grueneis2024,jmlm328 and mostly limited to very small monomers.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The aim of this paper is to assess different approximations which maintain the original CCSD(T) scaling, either by assessing the aforementioned CCSD(cT), CCSDT-2, and CCSDT-3 approaches, and/or by fitting different coefficients to these corrections to match CCSDT(Q) as closely as possible.
elements.sent-2411-013: We will also consider the CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$\cite{lambdastanton1,lambdastanton2,lambdabartlett1,lambdabartlett2 approach, which treats the triples as a perturbation to CCSD.}
elements.sent-2411-014: We have previously\cite{jmlm319 revised the CCSD(T) basis set limits for this dataset; in the present work, we shall attempt to evaluate post-CCSD(T) corrections for at least a representative subset of S66.}
elements.sent-2411-015: All electronic structure calculations in this work were carried out using a development version of the CFOUR \cite{CFOUR electronic structure program system, run on the CHEMFARM cluster of the Faculty of Chemistry at Weizmann.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The methods beyond standard CCSD(T)\cite{Rag89 include fully iterative CCSDT,\cite{Noga1986 the CCSDT-3 approximation thereto,\cite{Noga1987 CCSDT(Q),\cite{mrcc8 and CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$.\cite{lambdastanton1,lambdastanton2,lambdabartlett1,lambdabartlett2 (We note, e.g., Ref.\cite{Cremer2001 that the difference between CCSDT-2 and CCSDT-3 only sets in at sixth order in perturbation theory.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: It results from the effect on the $T_3$ amplitudes equation of single excitations, which is neglected in CCSDT-2 but included in CCSDT-3.) The basis sets used are of the correlation consistent\cite{Dunning1989,Kendall1992,Woon1993,Dunning2002 family.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The shorthand cc-pVDZ(d,s) refers to at most $d$ and $s$ functions, respectively, on nonhydrogen and hydrogen atoms (the full cc-pVDZ basis set would correspond to cc-pVDZ(d,p).
elements.sent-2411-019: The smallest basis set of this series without any polarized functions would thus correspond to cc-pVDZ(p,s).) Reference geometries from the S66\cite{s66x8 dataset were initially taken as-is from the BEGDB database.\cite{BEGDB These geometries were originally optimized at the BSSE corrected MP2/cc-pVTZ level without any symmetry, and typically only have $C_1$ symmetry, but in some cases, they are very close to structures with $C_s$, $C_{2v}$, or $C_{2h}$ symmetries.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In several cases (such as 24 benzene\ldots benzene and 25 pyridine\ldots pyridine parallel-displaced, as well as 34 pentane\ldots pentane), we resorted to treating the symmetrized structure in order to be able to bring the calculations to completion at all.
elements.sent-2411-021: Even so, they took over a month each on compute nodes with 52 cores and 768 GB RAM.
elements.sent-2411-022: No BSSE corrections were applied, as it was shown in a separate study\cite{jmlm333 on S66 that BSSE contributions to post-CCSD(T) correlation contributions are negligible.}

elements.sent-2411-023: Relevant data are presented in Table~\ref{tab:S66 , while Table~\ref{tab:stats offers performance statistics broken down into the four primary categories from Refs.\citenum{s66x8,jmlm268 : hydrogen bonds (systems 1--23), $\pi$-stacking (systems 24--33), pure London dispersion (systems 34--46), and mixed-influence complexes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: CCSDT-3$-$CCSDT and CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) differences larger than 0.05 kcal/mol are found for AcOH...AcOH, AcOH...uracil, benzene and pyridine dimers, as well as for the benzene-pyridine dimer.
elements.sent-2411-025: As expected, higher-order triples are antibonding, most pronouncedly so for $\pi$ stacks.
elements.sent-2411-026: CCSDT-3 appears to recover the lion's share of the higher-order triples effects: the RMS difference (RMSD) with CCSDT drops from 0.07 \kcalmol\ for CCSD(T) to 0.025 \kcalmol\ for CCSDT-3.
elements.sent-2411-027: The most pronounced improvement is seen for $\pi$ stacks, followed by mixed-influence systems.
elements.sent-2411-028: A detailed perturbation theoretical analysis of the differences between various approximate triple-excitations methods has been published by Cremer and coworkers;\cite{Cremer2001 specifically, \begin{equation}E[CCSDT-3]-E[{\rm CCSD(T)}] = E^{[5]}_{TQ}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is the perturbation parameter and $E^{[5] _{TQ}$ is the fifth-order triples-disconnected quadruples interaction term.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The CCSDT -- CCSDT-3 difference corresponds to the triples-triples interaction term, which again starts at fifth order: \begin{equation}E[CCSDT]-E[CCSDT-3] = E^{[5]}_{TT}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} $E^{[5] _{TQ}$ appears to be antibonding across the board.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The fifth-order connected triples-triples term $E^{[5] _{TT}$, on the other hand, is found to be somewhat antibonding for $\pi$ stacks, slightly bonding for London complexes, and almost nil for the two remaining categories.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Gr\"uneis \etal,\cite{Grueneis2024 in the ESI of their paper, found that the difference between CCSD(T) and their CCSD(cT) for a set of nine $\pi$-stacks is surprisingly well described by the following model: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Grueneis} E[(cT)]\approx \frac{E[(T)]}{a_1+a_2 \frac{E_2}{E_{\rm corr,CCSD}}}\end{equation} This equation can be slightly rearranged as follows, with \jmlm{$\gamma=1/a_1$ and $\alpha=a_2/a_1$}: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrueneisAlt} E[cT]-E[T]\approx E[T]\left(\frac{\gamma}{1+\alpha\left(\frac{E_2}{E_{\rm corr,CCSD}}-1\right)}-1\right)\end{equation} in which $\gamma$ is an optional scaling factor, and $\alpha$ can be interpreted as a regularization parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-032: If the ratio of \jmlm{the MP2 correlation energy $E_2$} to the CCSD correlation energy $E_{\rm corr,CCSD}$ is near unity, then the denominator will be largely unaffected; if, however, MP2 grossly overestimates the CCSD correlation energy because of near-degeneracy, then the denominator will `throttle' the quasiperturbative triples to compensate.
elements.sent-2411-033: Eq.\ref{eq:Grueneis , with \jmlm{parameters $a_1=0.9337$ and $a_2=0.1385$ fitted in this work , performs fairly poorly for $T_3-(T)$, its RMSD of 0.032 \kcalmol\ being about half of the actual value.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In contrast, CCSDT-3$-$CCSD(T) is modeled moderately well at RMSD=0.016 \kcalmol --- one-quarter of the actual effect, meaning the model recovers about three-quarters of the variation in it.
elements.sent-2411-035: However, the \jmlm{fitted model parameters $a_1=1.140$, $a_2=-0.016$ suggest that a simple scaling of (T) would work equally well, and indeed the same RMSD=0.016 \kcalmol\ is obtained by 0.895 $E$[(T)] for all of $E[CCSDT-3 ] - E[CCSD ]$, which works out to -0.105$E$[(T)] for CCSDT-3 -- CCSD(T) by itself.}
elements.sent-2411-036: CCSDT(Q) is exact to fifth order: the additional terms that (Q) introduces beyond CCSDT are \begin{equation}E[{\rm CCSDT(Q)}]-E[{\rm CCSDT}] = E^{[5]}_{QQ}+E^{[5]}_{QT}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} where $E^{[5] _{QQ}$ is the fifth-order connected quadruples term, and it should be noted that $E^{[5] _{QT}$ is the Hermitian conjugate of $E^{[5] _{QT}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Hence, for real orbitals, CCSD(T) differs from CCSDT(Q) by three fifth-order by including three extra terms: \begin{equation}E[{\rm CCSDT(Q)}]-E[{\rm CCSD(T)}] = E^{[5]}_{QQ}+2E^{[5]}_{TQ}+E^{[5]}_{TT}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} Now (Q) is attractive across the board, by 0.043 kcal/mol RMS, and unsurprisingly its greatest importance by far is for the $\pi$ stacks, as it compensates for the aforementioned strong effects found in the higher-order triples terms.
elements.sent-2411-038: An Eq.\ref{eq:Grueneis type model performs somewhat indifferently when fitted to the CCSDT(Q)-CCSDT differences, yielding an RMS=0.017 \kcalmol\ with $a_1$=0.847, $a_2$=0.052.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We could instead carry out a form of geometric extrapolation (a special case of Pad\'e approximants\cite{DavidGoodson ), in which we assume that $E_{\rm corr,CCSD}$, $E(T)$, and $E(Q)$ converge in a geometric series.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The latter implies, if we define the geometric decay ratio $r=E(T)/E_{\rm corr,CCSD}$, that $E(Q)\approx r.E(T)=E(T)^2/E_{\rm corr,CCSD}$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Summation to the full-CI limit would then lead to \begin{equation}E_{\rm FCI}-E_{\rm corr,CCSD}\approx E(T).(1+r+r^2+r^3+\ldots)=E(T)/(1-r)\end{equation} If we introduce two adjustable parameters, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:geom} E(Q)\approx E(T)\times \left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta \frac{E(T)}{E_{\rm corr}[{\rm CCSD}]}}-1\right)\end{equation} which for $\alpha$=1.127 and $\beta$=-0.168 has a somewhat lower RMSD=0.014 \kcalmol.
elements.sent-2411-042: Scaling the actual calculated $E$[(Q)] obtained in the unpolarized cc-pVDZ(p,s) basis set by 1.267 yields RMSD=0.011 kcal/mol, but still requires pretty costly calculations for larger systems.
elements.sent-2411-043: In contrast, a simple scaling of the CCSDT-3 -- CCSD(T) difference by -0.8249 achieves RMSD=0.011 \kcalmol.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Post-CCSD(T) contributions (\kcalmol) for nine $\pi$ stacks compared with earlier FN-DMC data.\label{tab:vsgrueneis}} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.9} \begin{tabular}{l|rc|rrrc} (cT)-(T) FN-DMC$-$CCSD(T)/CBS T-3$-$(T) $T_3-$(T) (Q) CCSDT(Q)$-$CCSD(T) Ref.\cite{Grueneis2024} Ref.\cite{Al-Hamdani2021}-Ref.\cite{Grueneis2024} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Present work} 24 Benzene \dots benzene ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.21 $-0.32\pm0.12$ -0.142 -0.204 0.121 -0.085 25 Pyridine \dots pyridine ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.23 $-0.26\pm0.20$ -0.145 -0.227 0.129 -0.099 26 Uracil \dots uracil ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.30 $-0.33\pm0.16$ -0.120 [+0.02] (0.10) (0.00) 27 Benzene \dots pyridine ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.22 $-0.28\pm0.16$ -0.144 -0.217 (0.12) (-0.08) 28 Benzene \dots uracil ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.27 $-0.45\pm0.18$ -0.144 [-0.17] (0.12) (-0.05) 29 Pyridine \dots uracil ($\pi\ldots\pi$) -0.27 $-0.29\pm0.18$ -0.149 [-0.19] (0.13) (-0.06) 47 Benzene \dots benzene (TS) -0.12 $-0.11\pm0.12$ -0.070 -0.095 (0.06) (-0.02) 48 Pyridine \dots pyridine (TS) -0.12 $-0.05\pm0.20$ -0.068 -0.096 (0.06) (-0.02) 49 Benzene \dots pyridine (TS) -0.12 $-0.20\pm0.16$ -0.068 -0.096 (0.06) (-0.02) \end{tabular} CBS=complete basis set (limit). Extrapolated CCSD(T)/CBS values taken from Table S I in Ref.\citenum{Grueneis2024}, FN-DMC values with uncertainties ($2\sigma$) from Table 1 in Ref.\citenum{Al-Hamdani2021}. These latter authors consider the difference between LNO-CCSD(T)/CBS and FN-DMC to be different from statistical zero only in cases 24 and 28, by $-0.1$ \kcalmol\ in both cases. Model estimates for (Q) and CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) are given in round parentheses. By subtracting the former form the latter estimates, we obtain the $T_3-(T)$ values given in square parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-044: Now let us consider the post-CCSD(T) correction as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-045: Both CCSD(T) and CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$ benefit from error compensation here, with RMSDs of 0.026 and 0.024, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-046: However, while CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$ clearly performs best for $\pi$ stacks, it somewhat `drops the ball' for pure London dispersion, while the opposite is true for CCSD(T).
elements.sent-2411-047: A combination Gr\"uneis and $E_5^{[TQ]}$ scaling model of the form \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:3parmodel} E[postCCSD(T)] \approx (T)[\frac{1}{a_1+a_2 \frac{E_2}{E_{\rm corr}[{\rm CCSD}]}}-1]\notag +a_3(E[CCSDT-3]-E[{\rm CCSD(T)}])\end{eqnarray} reaches a RMSD=0.009 \kcalmol\ with $a_1$=0.730, $a_2$=0.140, and $a_3$=0.968.
elements.sent-2411-048: The result is equivalent in quality: RMSD=0.009 \kcalmol, $a_1=0.727$, $a_2=0.140$, $a_3=1$.
elements.sent-2411-049: If we fix $a_3=1$, then RMSD is unchanged to three decimal places for $\alpha=1.139$, $\beta=-0.273$, which are effectively the parameters for a geometric-style fit of CCSDT(Q) - CCSDT-3.
elements.sent-2411-050: As a sanity check, we consider the recently reported\cite{jmlm335 reduced-scaling CCSDT(Q)\cite{Lesiuk21,Lesiuk22 calculations on the sandwich structure of naphthalene dimer.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: With the cc-pVDZ(d,p) basis set, these authors find a CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) difference of -0.67 \kJmol\ (-0.160 kcal/mol) for the naphthalene sandwich, and -0.32 \kJmol\ (-0.076 kcal/mol) for the corresponding benzene sandwich (which is well known\cite{Sherrill2004 to be a saddle point).}
elements.sent-2411-052: The corresponding estimates with Eq.\ref{eq:3parmodel are $-0.150$ and $-0.055$ \kcalmol, respectively, in excellent agreement with the calculated values.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Finally, Table \ref{tab:vsgrueneis presents a comparison between the present results and those obtained for nine complexes (six parallel-displaced $\pi$ stacks 24-29 and three T-shaped complexes 47-49) by Gr\"uneis and coworkers\cite{Grueneis2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Clearly, their quasiperturbative CCSD(cT) approach yields higher-order triples contributions remarkably close to full CCSDT and actually outperforms CCSDT-3 in this regard.
elements.sent-2411-055: Longer FN-DMC simulations with a smaller uncertainty would be highly desirable.
elements.sent-2411-056: We can spell out our conclusions here:.
elements.sent-2411-057: CCSD(T) and CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$ both benefit from error compensation and are both closer to CCSDT(Q) than either CCSDT-3 or full CCSDT.
elements.sent-2411-058: CCSD(T)$_\Lambda$ yields better results for $\pi$ stacks but slightly overbinds London complexes.
elements.sent-2411-059: This gap opens up further for naphthalene dimer, and can be expected to grow further for larger aromatic $\pi$-stacks.

elements.sent-2411-081: CCSDT-3$-$CCSDT and CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) differences larger than 0.05 \kcalmol\ are found for AcOH...AcOH, AcOH...uracil, benzene and pyridine dimers, as well as for all the aromatic-aromatic $\pi$ stacks, systems {\bf 24}\ldots{\bf 29 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: CCSDT-3 appears to recover the lion's share of the higher-order triples effects: the RMS difference (RMSD) with CCSDT drops from \jmlm{0.08 \kcalmol\ for CCSD(T) to \jmlm{0.029 \kcalmol\ for CCSDT-3.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Gr\"uneis \etal,\cite{Grueneis2024 in the Supporting Information of their paper, found that the difference between CCSD(T) and their CCSD(cT) for a set of nine $\pi$-stacks is surprisingly well described by the following model: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Grueneis} \Delta E[(cT)]\approx \frac{\Delta E[(T)]}{a_1+a_2 \frac{\Delta E_2}{\Delta E_{\rm corr,CCSD}}}\end{equation} This equation can be slightly rearranged as follows, with \jmlm{$\gamma=1/a_1$ and $\alpha=a_2/a_1$}: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrueneisAlt} \Delta E[cT]-\Delta E[(T)]\approx \Delta E[(T)]\left(\frac{\gamma}{1+\alpha\left(\frac{\Delta E_2}{\Delta E_{\rm corr,CCSD}}-1\right)}-1\right)\end{equation} in which $\gamma$ is an optional scaling factor and $\alpha$ can be interpreted as a regularization parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-084: If the ratio of \jmlm{the MP2 correlation energy $E_2$} to the CCSD correlation energy $E_{\rm corr,CCSD}$ is near unity, then the denominator will be largely unaffected; if, however, MP2 grossly overestimates the CCSD correlation energy because of near-degeneracy, then the denominator will `throttle' the quasiperturbative triples to compensate, with $\beta$ acting as the throttle lever.
elements.sent-2411-085: We note that nonlinear corrections like Eqs.(\ref{eq:Grueneis ,\ref{eq:GrueneisAlt ) as well as Eqs.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Eq.\ref{eq:Grueneis , \jmlm{with parameters $a_1=0.9417$ and $a_2=0.1442$ fitted in this work, performs fairly poorly for $T_3-(T)$, its RMSD of 0.035 \kcalmol\ being almost half of the actual effect, 0.083 kcal/mol RMS.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: However, the \jmlm{fitted model parameters $a_1=1.140$, $a_2=-0.016$ suggest that a simple scaling of (T) would work equally well, and indeed the same RMSD=0.016 \kcalmol\ is obtained by 0.895 $\Delta E$[(T)] for all of $\Delta E[CCSDT-3 ] - \Delta E[CCSD ]$, which works out to -0.105$E$[(T)] for CCSDT-3 -- CCSD(T) by itself.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Surprisingly, however, it turns out CCSDT$-$CCSD(T) can be modeled to RMSD=0.009 kcal/mol by also considering the CCSDT-1b method, which can be seen as an iterative counterpart to CCSD(T).
elements.sent-2411-090: This equation, being linear, is size-extensive to boot, at the expense of requiring an additional $O(n_{\rm occ ^3N_{\rm virt ^4)$ calculation step, but still having no steeper scaling than $O(n_{\rm occ ^3N_{\rm virt ^4)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: CCSDT(Q) is exact to fifth order: the additional terms that (Q) introduces beyond CCSDT are \begin{equation}E[{\rm CCSDT(Q)}]-E[{\rm CCSDT}] = E^{[5]}_{QQ}+E^{[5]}_{QT}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} where $E^{[5] _{QQ}$ is the fifth-order connected quadruples term, and it should be noted that the disconnected quadruples-triples term $E^{[5] _{QT}$ is the Hermitian conjugate of $E^{[5] _{TQ}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Hence, for real orbitals, CCSD(T) differs from CCSDT(Q) by three fifth-order terms: \begin{equation}E[{\rm CCSDT(Q)}]-E[{\rm CCSD(T)}] = E^{[5]}_{QQ}+2E^{[5]}_{TQ}+E^{[5]}_{TT}+O(\lambda^6)\end{equation} Now (Q) is attractive across the board, by 0.043 \kcalmol\ RMS, and unsurprisingly its greatest importance by far is for the $\pi$ stacks, as it compensates for the aforementioned strong effects found in the higher-order triples terms.
elements.sent-2411-093: Summation to the full-CI limit would then lead to \begin{equation}E_{\rm FCI}-E_{\rm corr,CCSD}\approx E(T)\times (1+r+r^2+r^3+\ldots)=E(T)/(1-r)\end{equation} If we introduce two adjustable parameters, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:geom} \Delta E(Q)\approx \Delta E(T)\times \left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta \frac{E(T)}{\Delta E_{\rm corr}[{\rm CCSD}]}}-1\right)\end{equation} which for $\alpha$=1.127 and $\beta$=-0.168 has a somewhat lower RMSD=0.014 \kcalmol.
elements.sent-2411-094: Scaling the actual calculated $\Delta E$[(Q)] obtained in the unpolarized cc-pVDZ(p,s) basis set by 1.267 yields RMSD=0.011 \kcalmol, but still requires pretty costly calculations for larger systems.
elements.sent-2411-095: In contrast, simple scaling of the CCSDT-3 -- CCSD(T) difference \begin{equation}\label{eq:parenQ} \Delta E(Q)\approx -b_1 (\Delta E[CCSDT-3] - \Delta E[CCSD(T)])\end{equation} with $b_1=0.825$ achieves RMSD=0.011 \kcalmol.
elements.sent-2411-096: Another way to look at this result is that most of the CCSDT(Q)$-$CCSD(T) difference will reflect CCSDT$-$CCSDT-3, since the two other components nearly cancel each other on average.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Dependence of parameters in Eq.(\ref{eq:geom3par}) on the basis set.\label{tab:eq10basis}} \begin{tabular}{l|rrrrr} cc-pVDZ(d,s) cc-pVDZ(d,p) cc-pVTZ(f,p) haVDZ(d,p) haVTZ(f,d) $\alpha$ 1.139 1.196 1.238 1.161 1.267 $\beta$ -0.273 -0.571 -0.824 -0.504 -1.009 RMSD (\kcalmol) 0.011 0.011 0.010 0.011 0.010 \end{tabular} $a_3$=1 throughout. The notation haV$n$Z stands for cc-pV$n$Z on hydrogen combined with aug-cc-pV$n$Z on the remaining elements.

Extrapolated CCSD(T)/CBS values taken from Table S I in Ref.\citenum{Grueneis2024}, FN-DMC values with uncertainties ($2\sigma$) from Table 1 in Ref.\citenum{Al-Hamdani2021}. These latter authors consider the difference between localized natural orbital coupled cluster, LNO-CCSD(T)/CBS and FN-DMC to be different from statistical zero only in cases 24 and 28, by $-0.1$ \kcalmol\ in both cases. Model estimates for (Q) and CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) are given in parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-097: A combination Gr\"uneis and $E_5^{[TQ]}$ scaling model of the form \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:3parmodel} \Delta E[ postCCSD(T)] \approx \Delta E(T)[\frac{1}{a_1+a_2 \frac{\Delta E_2}{\Delta E_{\rm corr}[{\rm CCSD}]}}-1]\notag +a_3(\Delta E[ CCSDT-3]-\Delta E[ CCSD(T)])\end{eqnarray} reaches a RMSD=0.009 \kcalmol\ with $a_1$=0.730, $a_2$=0.140, and $a_3$=0.968.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\label{eq:3parmodel} \Delta E[ postCCSD(T)] \approx \Delta E(T)[\frac{1}{a_1+a_2 \frac{\Delta E_2}{\Delta E_{\rm corr}[{\rm CCSD}]}}-1]\notag +a_3(\Delta E[ CCSDT-3]-\Delta E[ CCSD(T)])\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: If we fix $a_3=1$ --- which effectively makes the first term of Eq.(\ref{eq:geom3par ) a geometric-style fit of CCSDT(Q) - CCSDT-3 --- then RMSD is unchanged to three decimal places for $\alpha=1.139$, $\beta=-0.273$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Once again, if we additionally have CCSDT-1b available, this permits a size-consistent alternative: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:linearTQ} E[ postCCSD(T)] \approx\notag 1.085\left(E[CCSDT-3]-E[CCSD(T)]\right) \notag -1.515\left(E[CCSDT-1b]-E[CCSD(T)]\right) +0.200 E[(T)]\end{eqnarray} This equation yields RMSD=0.013 kcal/mol for CCSDT(Q)$-$CCSD(T), but system 22 is clearly an outlier as it was for the (Q) fit.
elements.sent-2411-100: Deleting 22 reduces RMSD to 0.009 kcal/mol; reoptimizing the parameters does not affect RMSD to the precision given, and the parameters change only minimally to \{1.090, $-$1.502, 0.204\}.
elements.sent-2411-101: With the cc-pVDZ(d,p) basis set, these authors find a CCSDT(Q)-CCSD(T) difference of -0.67 \kJmol\ (-0.160 \kcalmol) for the naphthalene sandwich, and -0.32 \kJmol\ (-0.076 \kcalmol) for the corresponding benzene sandwich (which is well known\cite{Sherrill2004 to be a saddle point).}
elements.sent-2411-102: Using Eq.(\ref{eq:geom3par ) we obtain estimates of $-0.150$ and $-0.055$ \kcalmol, respectively, while the corresponding values with Eq.(\ref{eq:linearTQ ) are $-0.201$ and $-0.089$ kcal/mol, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The two sets of estimates closely bracket the actual calculated values, at a tiny fraction of the latter's computational expense.

elements.sent-2411-113: These statistics imply that, despite post-CCSD(T) corrections in small-molecule thermochemistry being at least an order of magnitude more important than in noncovalent interactions, Eqs.(\ref{eq:geom3par ,\ref{eq:linear ) are still useful here as a semiquantitative estimate (and hence, as a fairly low-cost predictor of whether the more rigorous calculation will affect the species of interest signficantly).}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Already for small aromatic $\pi$-stacks in the S66 dataset, one observes nontrivial discrepancies between CCSD(T) and CCSDT(Q).
elements.sent-2411-115: The lion's share of $T_3-(T)$ is recovered at the CCSDT-3 level, and CCSDT-3 $-$ CCSD(T) can be well estimated from (T) by a simple two-parameter formula, while the same is possible for CCSDT $-$ CCSD(T) if CCSDT-1b and CCSDT-3 results are available.
elements.sent-2411-116: The best predictor of (Q) is a scaled CCSDT-3 $-$ CCSD(T) difference.
elements.sent-2411-117: The CCSDT(Q)$-$CCSD(T) difference can actually be reproduced to about 0.01 \kcalmol\ RMSD using a two-parameter formula, Eq.(\ref{eq:geom3par ) with $a_3$=1, or a three-parameters formula, Eq.(\ref{eq:linearTQ ).}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Neither requires more strenuous calculations than CCSDT-3, and hence no steeper scaling with system size than $O(N^7)$.
elements.sent-2411-119: As the revision of this manuscript was being finalized, a preprint reporting FN-DMC results for S66 came out.\cite{Zen2024S66 That study appears to indicate that, directly opposite to its behavior for $\pi$ stacks, FN-DMC actually binds hydrogen-bonded dimers more strongly than CCSD(T).}
elements.sent-2411-120: Reported discrepancies between FN-DMC and CCSD(T) reach as high as 0.7 kcal/mol for 20 AcOH\ldots\ AcOH --- these are very hard to rationalize as remaining flaws of CCSD(T), as Lao also very recently concluded.\cite{Lao2024.}
